reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware vent design judgment: non-monotonic efficiency vs. vent size, secondary-explosion risk at small vents, and configuration-specific optimality, while penalizing overgeneralization (e.g., “eliminates risk” or “optimal regardless of constraints”). Decoys are mostly plausible and hinge on single dominant mistakes, though one distractor bundles multiple errors, slightly reducing single-fault purity and discriminative sharpness.

The prompt cleanly targets two coupled engineering judgments (combined mechanical-thermal abuse changing TR initiation/severity classification, and delayed venting creating a secondary combustible-cloud hazard) with explicit scenario constraints that bound what can and cannot be concluded. It has strong discriminative power because an expert must separate thermal-only trigger logic from mechanically assisted initiation, and distinguish primary overpressure relief from the qualitatively different risk of late venting and external cloud ignition. Minor quality risk remains because “classified differently” can be interpreted as needing a formal taxonomy name and the vent description (fixed-area, high opening pressure) implicitly assumes enclosure tightness and ignition availability, which could invite small interpretive variance.
